title: Mixed conditional: past→present
question: If I had studied medicine, I ___ a doctor now.
explanation: This mixed conditional combines a past condition (if + past perfect) with a present result (would + base verb). The past choice affects the present situation.

title: Mixed conditional: past→present
question: If she hadn't missed her flight, she ___ here with us today.
explanation: A past event (missing the flight) affects the present situation. Use 'would + base verb' for the present result.

title: Mixed conditional: present→past
question: If he weren't so lazy, he ___ the project on time last week.
explanation: This mixed conditional combines a present condition (if + past simple) with a past result (would have + past participle).

title: Mixed conditional: present→past
question: If I spoke Chinese, I ___ the job they offered me last year.
explanation: The present condition (not speaking Chinese) affected a past outcome. Use 'would have + past participle' for the past result.

title: Mixed conditional: past→present
question: If we had bought that house in 2010, we ___ much richer now.
explanation: The past decision (not buying the house) has a present consequence. Use 'would + base verb' for the present result.

title: Mixed conditional: present→past
question: If I didn't have a fear of heights, I ___ with you last summer.
explanation: A permanent present condition (fear of heights) affected a past opportunity. Use 'would have + past participle' for the past result.

title: Mixed conditional: past→present
question: If they hadn't moved abroad, their children ___ English as well as they do.
explanation: The past action (moving abroad) created the present ability. Use 'would/wouldn't + base verb' for the present result.

title: Mixed conditional: present→past
question: If she weren't such a talented musician, she ___ the scholarship last year.
explanation: A present quality (being talented) led to a past result. Use 'would have + past participle' for the past outcome.

title: Mixed conditional: past→present
question: If I hadn't broken my leg last month, I ___ in the marathon tomorrow.
explanation: The past injury affects a present/near-future plan. 'Would be running' shows the ongoing present consequence of the past event.

title: Mixed conditional: present→past
question: If he weren't allergic to cats, he ___ the kitten his friend offered him.
explanation: A permanent present condition (allergy) affected a specific past opportunity. Use 'would have + past participle' for the past result.

title: Causative: have done
question: I need to ___ before the wedding.
explanation: The causative structure is 'have + object + past participle'. You arrange for someone else to do the service.

title: Causative: have done
question: She ___ every six months.
explanation: The causative 'have something done' means you arrange for a professional to do it. Structure: have + object + past participle.

title: Causative: get done
question: We're going to ___ this weekend.
explanation: 'Get something done' is an informal alternative to 'have something done'. Structure: get + object + past participle.

title: Causative: have done
question: He ___ last week after it broke down.
explanation: 'Had his car repaired' means he arranged for a mechanic to fix it. Structure: have + object + past participle.

title: Causative: get done
question: I'm getting ___ next Tuesday.
explanation: 'Get something done' follows the pattern: get + object + past participle. 'My hair cut' places the object before the participle.

title: Causative: have done
question: They ___ once a week by a professional.
explanation: The causative structure 'have + object + past participle' shows that someone else performs the action for you.

title: Causative: have vs do yourself
question: Which sentence means someone else fixed it? 'I ___ yesterday.'
explanation: 'Had my phone repaired' uses the causative — it means you arranged for someone else to do it. The other options mean you did it yourself.

title: Causative: get done (past)
question: She got ___ before her job interview.
explanation: 'Get something done' in the past: got + object + past participle. 'Done' is the past participle here.

title: Causative: have done (negative)
question: We should ___ before winter arrives.
explanation: Even in suggestions, the causative keeps its structure: have + object + past participle.

title: Causative: get done
question: He needs to get ___ because it keeps crashing.
explanation: 'Get something done': get + object + past participle. The object (his computer) comes before the participle (fixed).

title: Inversion: never
question: Never ___ such a beautiful sunset.
explanation: After 'never' at the start of a sentence, we invert the subject and auxiliary verb: Never + auxiliary + subject + main verb.

title: Inversion: seldom
question: Seldom ___ so many people at this event.
explanation: After negative adverbs like 'seldom' at the start, invert the auxiliary and subject: Seldom + does + subject + verb.

title: Inversion: rarely
question: Rarely ___ a student with such dedication.
explanation: After 'rarely' at the start of a sentence, use inverted word order: auxiliary + subject + main verb.

title: Inversion: not only
question: Not only ___ the exam, but she also got the highest score.
explanation: 'Not only' at the start of a sentence triggers inversion: Not only + auxiliary + subject + verb.

title: Inversion: no sooner
question: No sooner ___ the door than the phone rang.
explanation: 'No sooner...than' uses past perfect with inversion: No sooner + had + subject + past participle.

title: Inversion: hardly
question: Hardly ___ speaking when everyone started laughing.
explanation: 'Hardly' at the start triggers inversion with past perfect: Hardly + had + subject + past participle.

title: Inversion: under no circumstances
question: Under no circumstances ___ this building without permission.
explanation: After negative expressions like 'under no circumstances', the auxiliary verb comes before the subject.

title: Inversion: at no time
question: At no time ___ aware of the danger.
explanation: After 'at no time', invert auxiliary and subject: At no time + were + subject + complement.

title: Inversion: little
question: Little ___ that it would be her last performance.
explanation: After 'little' used as a negative adverb at the start, inversion is required: Little + did + subject + base verb.

title: Inversion: only after
question: Only after the meeting ___ the importance of the decision.
explanation: 'Only after' at the start of a sentence triggers inversion in the main clause: Only after X + auxiliary + subject + verb.

title: Phrasal verb: literal vs idiomatic
question: The hikers had to ___ early because of the storm. (= return)
explanation: 'Turn back' means to return the way you came. 'Turn up' means to appear or arrive. 'Turn down' means to refuse. 'Turn over' means to flip something.

title: Phrasal verb: idiomatic
question: I can't ___ what he's saying — the music is too loud. (= understand/hear)
explanation: 'Make out' means to understand or hear something with difficulty. 'Make up' means to invent. 'Make off' means to leave quickly. 'Make over' means to renovate.

title: Phrasal verb: idiomatic
question: She's been ___ the idea of starting her own business. (= considering)
explanation: 'Toy with an idea' means to consider it casually. 'Play out' means to develop. 'Run into' means to encounter. 'Go through' means to experience.

title: Phrasal verb: literal vs idiomatic
question: The meeting was ___ until next week. (= postponed)
explanation: 'Put off' means to postpone. 'Put up' means to accommodate or raise. 'Put out' means to extinguish. 'Put down' means to criticise or write.

title: Phrasal verb: idiomatic
question: Sales have really ___ since we launched the new campaign. (= increased rapidly)
explanation: 'Take off' can mean to increase rapidly (idiomatic). 'Take in' means to absorb or deceive. 'Take on' means to accept. 'Take over' means to gain control.

title: Phrasal verb: idiomatic
question: We need to ___ a solution before the deadline. (= think of/produce)
explanation: 'Come up with' means to think of or produce an idea or solution. 'Come across' means to find by chance. 'Come down with' means to become ill. 'Come round to' means to eventually agree.

title: Phrasal verb: idiomatic
question: The company had to ___ dozens of workers due to budget cuts. (= dismiss from employment)
explanation: 'Lay off' means to dismiss workers, usually for economic reasons. 'Lay into' means to attack verbally. 'Lay down' means to establish rules. 'Lay out' means to arrange or display.

title: Phrasal verb: literal vs idiomatic
question: He ___ his father — they have the same eyes and smile. (= resembles)
explanation: 'Take after' means to resemble a family member. 'Take up' means to start a hobby. 'Take in' means to absorb. 'Take to' means to begin to like.

title: Phrasal verb: idiomatic
question: I need to ___ this report before submitting it. (= review)
explanation: 'Go over' means to review or check. 'Go off' means to explode or stop liking. 'Go about' means to approach a task. 'Go through with' means to complete despite difficulty.

title: Phrasal verb: idiomatic
question: She ___ a cold just before her holiday. (= became ill with)
explanation: 'Come down with' means to become ill with something. 'Come up with' means to think of. 'Come across' means to find by chance. 'Come round to' means to eventually agree.

title: Cleft: It is...that
question: ___ the manager who approved the changes, not the director.
explanation: Cleft sentences use 'It is/was...who/that' to emphasise a particular element. Here the emphasis is on who approved the changes.

title: Cleft: What...is
question: ___ a good night's sleep.
explanation: 'What' introduces a cleft sentence to emphasise the object: What + subject + verb + is/was + emphasised element.

title: Cleft: It is...that
question: ___ his attitude that annoys me, not his work.
explanation: 'It is...that' cleft sentences emphasise a specific element. Here the emphasis is on 'his attitude' as the thing that annoys.

title: Cleft: What...was
question: ___ was the salary, not the location.
correct: What convinced me to take the job
wrong1: That convinced me to take the job
wrong2: It convinced me to take the job
wrong3: Which convinced me to take the job
explanation: 'What + clause + was' is a cleft structure that emphasises the complement. Here it highlights the salary as the convincing factor.

title: Cleft: What...was
question: We don't need more money. ___ more time.
explanation: 'What we need is...' uses a what-cleft to emphasise 'more time'. 'What' introduces the clause and 'is' links to the focus.

title: Cleft: It was...that
question: ___ in Paris that they first met, not in London.
explanation: 'It was...that' emphasises the location. The sentence stresses where they met by placing 'in Paris' in the focus position.

title: Cleft: What...is
question: ___ her constant complaining.
explanation: The what-cleft 'What + verb phrase + is/was' emphasises the complement. Here it highlights what specifically bothers the speaker.

title: Cleft: It is...who
question: ___ should apologise, not you.
explanation: 'It is...who' emphasises the person. This cleft sentence stresses that he — not you — should apologise.

title: Cleft: What...did
question: ___ was resign without notice.
explanation: 'What + subject + did + was + base verb' is a cleft that emphasises the action. It highlights 'resigning without notice' as the key action.

title: Cleft: All...is
question: I'm not asking for much. ___ a little patience.
explanation: 'All + subject + verb + is' works like a cleft sentence to emphasise that the request is simple: just a little patience.
